GS3.headline: ISRO Accelerates Preparations for Chandrayaan-4 Mission to Moon's South Pole
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced accelerated preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, targeting the lunar South Pole.
• Chandrayaan-4 aims to further explore the potential for water ice and conduct in-situ analysis of lunar soil and rocks.
• The mission is expected to feature a more advanced rover and lander system, building upon the successes and lessons from Chandrayaan-3.
• This mission reinforces India's growing capabilities in lunar exploration and its commitment to contributing to global space science.
• Scientists anticipate crucial data from Chandrayaan-4 that could inform future human missions to the Moon.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology-developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space
prelimsPoints: Chandrayaan-4 is India's next lunar mission.
Its primary target is the Moon's South Pole.
The mission aims to study water ice and lunar geology.
ISRO is the agency responsible for the mission.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's contributions to lunar exploration and its significance for global space research.
Analyze the strategic importance of exploring the lunar South Pole for future space missions and resource utilization.
Examine the technological advancements and challenges involved in developing advanced lunar lander and rover systems.
Evaluate how India's space program fosters scientific temper and technological innovation within the country.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the Chandrayaan-4 lander and rover on the lunar surface.
GS3.trivia: India was the first country to successfully land a spacecraft near the Moon's South Pole with Chandrayaan-3.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Easing Inflation Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly review, deciding to maintain the repo rate at its current level.
• This decision comes as inflation shows signs of moderation, though remaining within the target band, allowing the RBI to prioritize growth.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to calibrating monetary policy to ensure price stability while supporting economic recovery.
• Experts suggest the RBI is adopting a cautious 'wait and watch' approach, monitoring global economic headwinds and domestic demand conditions.
• The unchanged repo rate is expected to provide stability to lending rates and encourage investment, supporting the broader economic sentiment.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: The RBI's MPC sets the repo rate.
Repo rate is the rate at which commercial banks borrow money from RBI.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation is a key factor influencing MPC decisions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the MPC in balancing inflation control with economic growth objectives.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing contemporary economic issues like supply-side inflation and global financial volatility.
Examine the impact of the RBI's monetary policy stance on various sectors of the economy, including banking, industry, and consumers.
GS3.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India headquarters building with its logo.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in India in 2016 and consists of six members.

GS3.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Shows Promising Progress in Pilot Projects
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy released a progress report on the National Green Hydrogen Mission, highlighting significant strides in pilot projects.
• Several industrial clusters and research institutions have successfully demonstrated green hydrogen production and application technologies.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, reducing dependence on fossil fuels.
• Key initiatives include incentives for electrolyzer manufacturing and development of green hydrogen corridors.
• This progress is crucial for India's energy transition goals and its commitments under the Paris Agreement.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Science and Technology-developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen.
Green hydrogen is produced using renewable energy through electrolysis.
It is crucial for decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors.
The mission provides incentives for manufacturing electrolyzers.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate goals.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption in India.
Evaluate the potential impact of the National Green Hydrogen Mission on India's industrial landscape and export capabilities.
Examine the role of government policies and international collaborations in fostering a green hydrogen ecosystem.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic illustration showing the process of green hydrogen production using solar and wind energy.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe.

GS3.headline: Report Highlights Growing Threat of Urban Heat Islands in Indian Cities
GS3.explainer:
• A new report by the National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA) detailed the increasing intensity and spread of Urban Heat Islands (UHIs) across major Indian cities.
• UHIs are phenomena where urban areas experience significantly higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas due to human activities and infrastructure.
• The report identifies factors like dense concrete structures, lack of green spaces, and vehicular emissions as primary contributors.
• Consequences include increased energy consumption for cooling, adverse health impacts, and reduced quality of life for urban dwellers.
• Recommendations include promoting green infrastructure, using reflective building materials, and integrating UHI mitigation strategies into urban planning.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
prelimsPoints: Urban Heat Islands (UHIs) are urban areas significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas.
Factors contributing to UHIs include concrete surfaces and lack of vegetation.
UHIs lead to increased energy use and health issues.
Mitigation strategies involve green roofs, cool pavements, and urban forestry.
mainsPoints: Discuss the causes and environmental consequences of Urban Heat Islands in Indian cities.
Analyze the socio-economic and public health impacts of rising urban temperatures.
Examine the role of urban planning and policy interventions in mitigating the UHI effect.
Suggest sustainable strategies for building climate-resilient cities in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A satellite image showing temperature variations, with red spots indicating urban heat islands.
GS3.trivia: The 'albedo effect' refers to how much sunlight a surface reflects; light-colored surfaces have a high albedo and reflect more heat.

GS3.headline: India Bolsters Border Infrastructure Along Eastern Sector Amidst Geopolitical Shifts
GS3.explainer:
• India has significantly ramped up its border infrastructure development along the eastern sector, particularly focusing on connectivity and surveillance.
• This strategic push is aimed at enhancing military mobility, improving logistics, and ensuring robust defense capabilities in challenging terrains.
• The projects include the construction of new roads, bridges, tunnels, and advanced surveillance systems.
• The move is seen as a response to evolving geopolitical dynamics and security challenges in the region, including activities by neighboring countries.
• It also aims to improve connectivity for local populations and foster economic development in remote border areas.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: India is developing infrastructure in its eastern border areas.
Projects include roads, bridges, and tunnels.
The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) is a key agency involved.
This enhances military mobility and surveillance capabilities.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic rationale behind India's intensified border infrastructure development, particularly in the eastern sector.
Analyze the challenges involved in constructing infrastructure in mountainous and remote border regions.
Examine how improved border infrastructure contributes to national security and regional stability.
Evaluate the socio-economic impact of such projects on the lives of border area populations.
GS3.imageDescription: Newly constructed road winding through mountainous terrain in a border region.
GS3.trivia: The Atal Tunnel, located in the Pir Panjal range, is the longest highway single-tube tunnel above 10,000 feet (3,000 meters) in the world.

GS3.headline: India Expands Wetland Conservation Efforts, Focus on Ramsar Sites Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announced enhanced efforts for wetland conservation and management, particularly focusing on India's Ramsar Sites.
• New guidelines emphasize integrated management plans, community participation, and scientific monitoring for ecological restoration.
• Wetlands are crucial ecosystems, providing vital services like water purification, flood control, and biodiversity habitats.
• The initiative aims to address threats such as pollution, encroachment, and climate change impacts on these fragile ecosystems.
• India's growing number of Ramsar sites underscores its commitment to international conservation agreements.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity and its conservation
Environmental Impact Assessment
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)
prelimsPoints: Ramsar Sites are wetlands of international importance designated under the Ramsar Convention.
Wetlands provide ecosystem services like water purification and flood control.
India has a significant number of Ramsar sites.
Conservation efforts focus on integrated management and community involvement.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of wetlands and the threats they face in India.
Analyze the importance of the Ramsar Convention and India's role in wetland conservation.
Examine the challenges in implementing effective management plans for Ramsar Sites, including issues of encroachment and pollution.
Suggest strategies for community participation and sustainable utilization of wetland resources.
GS3.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds, indicating rich biodiversity.
GS3.trivia: The Ramsar Convention on Wetlands was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971.

GS3.headline: Government Consults Stakeholders on AI Ethics and Governance Framework
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) initiated a consultation process with industry, academia, and civil society on developing a national framework for Artificial Intelligence (AI) ethics and governance.
• The framework aims to ensure responsible development and deployment of AI technologies, addressing concerns around bias, privacy, accountability, and human rights.
• Key principles under discussion include fairness, transparency, security, and explainability in AI systems.
• This proactive step positions India at the forefront of global efforts to shape ethical AI guidelines, fostering public trust and innovation.
• The framework will be crucial for guiding future AI policies and regulations across various sectors.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology-developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
IT, Computers, Robotics, Nanotechnology, Bio-technology and issues relating to Intellectual Property Rights
Cyber Security
GS3.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
prelimsPoints: India is developing a national framework for AI ethics and governance.
The framework addresses concerns like bias, privacy, and accountability in AI.
Key principles include fairness, transparency, and security.
MeitY is leading the consultation process.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges and societal implications arising from the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence.
Analyze the need for a comprehensive AI ethics and governance framework in India, considering its diverse population and digital aspirations.
Examine the key principles that should underpin a responsible AI framework, such as fairness, transparency, and accountability.
Evaluate India's potential role in shaping global norms and standards for ethical AI development.
GS3.imageDescription: Interconnected human brains and circuit boards, symbolizing the intersection of humanity and AI.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

GS3.headline: Government Raises Agriculture Credit Target to Boost Rural Economy
GS3.explainer:
• The government announced an enhanced agriculture credit target for the upcoming fiscal year, aiming to provide easier and affordable credit to farmers.
• This move is designed to boost agricultural productivity, support allied sectors, and strengthen the rural economy.
• Focus areas include providing credit for crop diversification, farm mechanization, and value-added agricultural activities.
• Challenges remain in ensuring equitable access to credit for small and marginal farmers, and addressing issues of land tenancy and collateral.
• The initiative is part of broader efforts to double farmers' income and promote sustainable agricultural practices.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management
prelimsPoints: Agriculture credit is crucial for the farm sector's growth.
Government raises targets to ensure affordable credit access.
Focus on crop diversification and farm mechanization.
Small and marginal farmers often face challenges in accessing credit.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of agriculture credit in promoting agricultural growth and ensuring farmers' welfare in India.
Analyze the challenges faced by small and marginal farmers in accessing institutional credit and suggest solutions.
Examine the role of government schemes and policies in enhancing agricultural credit availability and reducing rural indebtedness.
Evaluate the impact of increased credit on crop diversification, farm mechanization, and the overall rural economy.
GS3.imageDescription: A farmer working in a field with a tractor, symbolizing modern agriculture and credit support.
GS3.trivia: The Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme was introduced in 1998 to provide farmers with timely and adequate credit.

GS3.headline: India's Climate Change Vulnerability Index Underlines Regional Disparities
GS3.explainer:
• A comprehensive Climate Change Vulnerability Index (CCVI) for India revealed significant regional disparities in susceptibility to climate impacts.
• States in the Himalayan region and coastal areas were identified as highly vulnerable due to extreme weather events and rising sea levels.
• The index considers factors like exposure to climate hazards, sensitivity of socio-economic systems, and adaptive capacity of communities.
• This data is crucial for targeted policy interventions and allocation of resources for climate adaptation and resilience building.
• The report emphasizes the need for localized climate action plans to address specific vulnerabilities of different regions.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
Climate change
keyTerms: National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)
prelimsPoints: A Climate Change Vulnerability Index (CCVI) assesses regions' susceptibility to climate impacts.
Himalayan and coastal regions are often highly vulnerable.
Factors include exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity.
The index helps in developing targeted climate action plans.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of climate change vulnerability and its relevance for India.
Analyze the regional disparities in climate vulnerability across India and their underlying causes.
Examine the socio-economic and environmental consequences of high climate vulnerability in different regions.
Suggest policy measures and adaptation strategies to enhance climate resilience and reduce vulnerability in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A map of India colored to show different levels of climate change vulnerability across states.
GS3.trivia: India is a signatory to the Paris Agreement, which aims to limit global warming to well below 2, preferably to 1.5 degrees Celsius, compared to pre-industrial levels.

GS3.headline: Indian Startup Ecosystem Continues Robust Growth, Focus on Deep Tech and Rural Innovation
GS3.explainer:
• The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) released data indicating continued robust growth in India's startup ecosystem.
• A notable trend is the increasing investment and innovation in deep tech sectors like AI, advanced materials, and quantum computing.
• The government's 'Startup India' initiative is now also focusing on fostering innovation in Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities and rural areas.
• Challenges include access to early-stage funding, mentorship, and navigating regulatory complexities for nascent startups.
• This growth is vital for job creation, economic diversification, and positioning India as a global innovation hub.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology-developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
GS3.source: Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)
prelimsPoints: India has a rapidly growing startup ecosystem.
Deep tech refers to technologies based on substantial scientific discoveries or engineering innovations.
Startup India initiative promotes entrepreneurship.
Focus on Tier 2/3 cities and rural innovation is increasing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to the robust growth of India's startup ecosystem and its significance for the economy.
Analyze the role of 'Deep Tech' startups in driving innovation and competitiveness in the Indian context.
Examine the challenges faced by startups in India, especially those in Tier 2/3 cities and rural areas.
Suggest policy measures to further strengthen the startup ecosystem, including access to funding and regulatory support.
GS3.imageDescription: A group of young entrepreneurs collaborating in a modern co-working space, symbolizing innovation.
GS3.trivia: India is the third-largest startup ecosystem in the world, after the US and China.
